content: # Enhanced thermal stability of nanograined metals below a critical grain size
X. Zhou, X. Y. Li,* K. Lu*
# Enhanced thermal stability of nano-grained metals below a critical grain size
X. Zhou,1,2 X.Y. Li,1 and K. Lu1

1 Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science, Institute of Metal Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 72 Wenhua Road, Shenyang 110016, China   
2 School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

1. Materials   
2. Structure characterization   
3. Thermal annealing treatment   
4. Nano-hardness measurement   
5. Determination of grain boundary energy   
6. Composition analysis   
7. Texture analysis

Pure Cu (99.97 wt. $\%$ in purity) and pure Ni (99.5 wt. $\%$ in purity) were annealed at $4 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $1 0 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for $^ { 2 \mathrm { ~ h ~ } }$ , respectively, to form fully recrystallized structures. The rods of Cu with a diameter of $1 0 \mathrm { m m }$ and Ni with a diameter of 9 mm were processed by using surface mechanical grinding treatment (SMGT) (28) in liquid nitrogen at 77 K with processing parameters listed in Table S1. For each sample, the SMGT process was repeated several times for achieving a thick and uniform gradient nano-grained surface layer. The processed surface is very smooth with a roughness of $\mathtt { R a } { = } 0 . 3 ~ \mu \mathrm { m }$ . No surface crack was identified in the as-SMGT samples.

Structural characterization of the SMGT and the annealed samples were carried out on scanning electron microscopes (SEM, FEI Nova 430 and Verios 460) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20), respectively. Texture components in the surface layer of the as-SMGT Cu were determined by using TEM and EBSD equipped in SEM, respectively. The processed surface was protected by electrodepositing a thin Cu layer (for Cu samples) and Ni layer (for Ni samples), respectively. Cross-sectional sample preparation for SEM and TEM observations follows the ordinary route of cutting, mechanical polishing, and final electro-chemical polishing or thinning. The final finishing was accomplished by electro-chemical method using an electrolyte consisting of $2 5 \%$ alcohol, $2 5 \%$ phosphorus acid and $5 0 \%$ de-ionized water at $0 \ { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for Cu and an electrolyte consisting of $3 7 . 5 \%$ alcohol, $1 2 . 5 \%$ perchloric acid and $5 0 \%$ acetic acid at ${ } _ { - 2 0 } \mathrm { \Omega } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for Ni.

Three types of thermal annealing treatment were performed for characterize the grain size effect on thermal stability of nano-grained Cu and Ni samples. The annealing treatments were carried out in a tube furnace with protecting Ar atmosphere and a temperature control accuracy of $\pm 2 \mathrm { \textrm { K } }$ .

a) Step-wise annealing followed by ex-situ SEM observations: The as-SMGT Cu sample was coated by an electrodeposited Cu thin layer and a cross-sectional sample was prepared by cutting and polishing ready for SEM observations. The sample was firstly annealed in Ar atmosphere at $3 7 3 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }$ for $3 0 \ \mathrm { m i n }$ before cooling for SEM observations of the same area in the cross-sectional sample. After SEM observation, the sample was reheated up to 393 K, holding for 30 min and cooling down for another SEM observation. This procedure was repeated for several times to a final annealing temperature of 523 K, each time with a temperature increment of $2 0 ~ \mathrm { K }$ .

b) Direct heating and annealing at preset temperatures: The as-SMGT samples was heated rapidly $( \sim 1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K / m i n ) }$ to different preset temperatures, holding for 30 min and cooling down for SEM and TEM observations, respectively. For Cu, the annealing temperatures are: 373, 393, 413, 433, 453, 473, 523, 573, and $6 2 3 \mathrm { K }$ , respectively (Fig. S1). For Ni, the annealing temperatures are 573, 673, 773, 873, 973, 1073, and 1173 K, respectively (Fig. S2). Both Fig. S1 and S2 showed that some nano-grains in the surface layer are stable without coarsening at elevated temperatures while significant coarsening occurred in the submicro-sized grains.

c) Isothermal annealing: The as-SMGT Cu sample was heated up to $4 3 3 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }$ and hold for different durations (0.5, 1, 2, 5, and 12 hours, respectively) for study the effect of annealing time on grain coarsening (Fig. S3). The nano-grains in the surface layer are stable during annealing at this temperature for 12 hours.

Nano-hardness tests of samples before and after annealing were performed on a Hysitron Tribo-indenter. Measurement conditions include: a preset load of $3 0 0 0 \mu \mathrm { N }$ , a loading and unloading rate of $6 0 0 \mu \mathrm { N } / \mathrm { s }$ , and a holding time of 2 seconds. Nano-hardness values were averaged with at least 10 indentations. The spacing between two neighboring indents is above $1 5 \mu \mathrm { m }$ to avoid the overlapping effect.

The excess energy of grain boundary (GB) in the nano-grained surface layer of the SMGT Cu samples was determined by using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) on a TA DSC at a heating rate of $4 0 ~ \mathrm { K / m i n }$ . Several samples with different average grain sizes were measured. The top surface layer of ${ \sim } 1 5 \mu \mathrm { m }$ and ${ \sim } 8 ~ \mu \mathrm { m }$ thick, in which the average grain sizes are $5 0 { \pm } 1 2 ~ \mathrm { n m }$ and $4 5 { \pm } 5 \ \mathrm { n m }$ , respectively, were peeled off from the as-prepared SMGT sample for the DSC measurements. The subsurface layers with

average grain sizes of ${ \sim } 1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { n m }$ and ${ \sim } 1 2 5 ~ \mathrm { n m }$ were also prepared for measurements, respectively. Each sample was scanned twice for an accurate determination of the exothermal enthalpy released from grain coarsening. With the measured exothermal enthalpy $( \Delta H )$ , the GB excess energy (γ) can be estimated by $\Delta H { = } \Delta S { \cdot } \gamma$ , where $\Delta S$ is the change of GB area per unit mass, which is determined by the grain size change measured from TEM and SEM observations.

The composition analysis was carefully performed by using EDAX and EELS equipped in a FEI Tecnai F30 TEM and a FEI Tecnai F20 TEM to check the possible contamination in the top surface layer of SMGT samples. The EDAX line scanning results showed no obvious carbon or oxygen can be detected in the top surface layer and no obvious variations along the depth, as shown in Fig. S4. No obvious impurities were detected in EELS analysis of light elements in the top surface layer as depth exceeds $5 0 0 \mathrm { n m }$ in the as-SMGT Cu sample.

In order to eliminate the effect of possible processing-induced contamination in the surface layer, we used the same SMGT process but lower rotation speed (20 rpm) and feed rate $\mathrm { 1 ~ m m / m i n } )$ ) at $1 7 3 \ \mathrm { K }$ to prepare a submicro-grained surface layer. Compared with the process for the nano-grained surface layer, lower strain rates (but comparable accumulated plastic strain) and higher temperature resulted in larger grain sizes, averagely $1 4 0 \mathrm { n m }$ in the top surface layer. The level impurity induced by SMGT process in the top surface layer with submicro-grains is comparable to that with nano-grains. The submicro-grained sample was annealed at $4 3 3 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }$ for $3 0 \ \mathrm { m i n }$ and characterized under SEM. As shown in Fig. 2, grain coarsening appears in the topmost surface layer, rather than in the subsurface layer as seen in the nano-grained surface layer. This is a direct evidence showing that the high thermal stability of the top surface layer in the SMGT sample is a nano-scale grain size effect, rather than a result of processinginduced contamination.

Texture of the top surface layer of about $4 0 ~ { \mu \mathrm { m } }$ thick was examined in the crosssectional as-SMGT Cu sample by using EBSD mapping and electron diffraction under TEM. A weak texture $\{ 1 1 1 \} { < } 1 1 0 { > }$ (counted within $\pm 1 5 ^ { \circ }$ ) was identified from the pole figure {111} (Fig. S5). The fraction of $\{ 1 1 1 \} { < } 1 1 0 { > }$ texture is calculated at an intervals of $5 ~ { \mu \mathrm { m } }$ thick, which varies from $3 7 . 3 \%$ at the topmost surface to $3 1 . 5 \%$ at about 40 $\mu \mathrm { m }$ deep, meaning the texture is uniform along depth from surface. The IPF image also shows that the orientation distribution is relatively uniform in the top surface layer of Cu (Fig. S5).
Table S1. The parameters used in the SMGT process for Cu and Ni samples.

( $\pmb { v } _ { 1 }$ : rotating velocity of the sample; $v _ { 2 }$ : sliding velocity of the tool tips; $a _ { \mathsf { p } }$ : the preset penetration depth of the tool tip into the sample surface; r: diameter of the hemispherical WC/Co tool tip.)

Figure S1. Cross-sectional SEM images of the as-prepared gradient nano-grained Cu sample annealed directly at various temperatures (as indicated) for 30 min. Red dotted line outlined the recrystallized grains in the top surface layer.

Figure S2. A cross-sectional SEM image of the as-prepared gradient nano-grained Ni sample annealed at 1173 K for 30 min. (left). Red dotted lines outlined the recrystallized grains in the top surface layer. The area in the white-line square is magnified (right), showing presence of nano-sized grains sandwiched by coarsened grains.
Figure S3. Cross-sectional SEM images of the as-prepared gradient nano-grained Cu sample annealed at 433 K for different durations as indicated.
Figure S4. EDAX line scanning analysis in the top surface layer of the as-prepared gradient nano-grained Cu and Ni samples. Red lines indicated the treated surface.

Figure S5. Typical {111} pole figures showing a weak texture $\{ 1 1 1 \} { < } 1 1 0 { > }$ in the top layer of $0 { \cdot } 1 5 ~ { \mu } \mathsf { m }$ depth (upper left) and in a depth span of $1 5 { \cdot } 4 0 ~ { \mu \mathrm { m } }$ (lower left), as well as the EBSD image (middle) and the corresponding texture component $\{ 1 1 1 \} { < } 1 1 0 { > }$ map (right) in the top $0 { \cdot } 4 0 \mu \mathrm { m }$ surface layer in the as-prepared gradient nano-grained Cu sample.

Fig S6. Typical bright-field TEM images in the top $3 0 \mu \mathrm { m }$ layer $\sim 1 0 \mu \mathrm { m }$ depth layer, left) and $3 0 \mathrm { - } 6 0 \mu \mathrm { m }$ deep layer $\mathtt { \sim } 4 0 \mu \mathrm { m }$ depth layer, right) in the as-prepared gradient nanograined Ni sample; The fraction of grains containing twins or stacking faults is $3 8 . 9 \pm$ $5 \%$ in the top $3 0 \mu \mathrm { m }$ layer and $8 . 6 \pm 2 \%$ in $3 0 \mathrm { - } 6 0 \mu \mathrm { m }$ deep layer respectively.
